studies.title: IMPACT — Observational Study on Immunoadsorption (IA) in Patients With Autoantibody-Positive Post-Infectious ME/CFS
studies.location: Charité - Universitätsmedizin Berlin, Institute for Medical Immunology, Berlin

studies.title: IA-Surv — Observational study on immune adsorption (IA) in patients with GPCR autoantibody-positive post-COVID/post-vac syndrome (IA-Surv)
RAPID_REVIVE — Randomized Adaptive Assessment of Post-COVID Syndrome Treatments — REVIVE arm (vidofludimus calcium)
studies.location: University Hospital Frankfurt (sponsor / coordinating), Frankfurt am Main +1 more

studies.title: MDC002 Phase 1 Clinical Trial in ME/CFS (planned)

papers.title: Indistinguishable mitochondrial phenotypes after exposure of healthy myoblasts to myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome or control serum
papers.authors: Ryback AA, Hillier CB, Loureiro CM, Ponting CP, Dalton CF
papers.summary: A large, carefully pre-planned study tried to repeat a well-known 2016 finding that blood serum from ME/CFS patients changes how healthy muscle cells produce energy. Using blood from 67 patients and 53 healthy people and over 1,700 lab measurements, they found no such difference this time.
papers.why: A rigorous negative replication from Chris Ponting's group (who also leads the DecodeME genetics study already in this database) — it challenges a widely-cited piece of evidence for a 'blood-borne factor' in ME/CFS and shows the field is actively re-testing its own key claims.

papers.title: Exploring differences in protein cargo of extracellular vesicles from ME/CFS patient plasma compared to healthy controls
papers.authors: Rydland A, Yran ES, Nyman TA, Strand EB, Trøseid AS, Øvstebø R, Heinicke F, Lie BA, Viken MK
papers.summary: Looked at tiny particles that cells shed into the blood (extracellular vesicles) in 49 ME/CFS patients versus 50 healthy people. Patients had more of these particles overall, and a handful of the proteins they carried differed, though none of those individual differences held up after strict statistical checking.
papers.why: The largest such extracellular-vesicle study in ME/CFS so far; a mostly exploratory result, but it adds a new (Norwegian) patient group to this growing biomarker approach, alongside similar work already in this database from the Scheibenbogen group.

papers.title: Toward a Molecular Reclassification of Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome: Integrating Multi-Omics, Machine Learning, and Precision Medicine
papers.authors: Frank J, Nesterovitch N, Movva C, Klimas NG, Nathanson L
papers.summary: Reviews how new technologies that can read thousands of gene and protein signals at once are being used to sort ME/CFS patients into distinct biological groups — defined by what is going wrong in their bodies — rather than by symptoms alone, using artificial intelligence to find the patterns.
papers.why: Lays out a roadmap for 'precision medicine' in ME/CFS — where each patient subgroup receives treatment matched to their specific biology — and identifies overactive innate immunity, exhausted immune T cells, and cellular energy failure as the likeliest targets for the first such treatments. From leading US ME/CFS researcher Nancy Klimas's group.

papers.title: Comparative Gut Microbiome Alterations in Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome and Long COVID-19 Syndrome
papers.authors: Donchev D, Nikolova R, Vaseva K, Taskov H, Murdjeva M, Maes M, Ivanov IN
papers.summary: Directly compared the gut bacteria of ME/CFS patients and long COVID patients, finding shared changes — particularly missing 'good' bacteria and excess of others — that were not seen in healthy people.
papers.why: The first head-to-head comparison of gut microbiome changes in ME/CFS and long COVID; the overlap supports the idea that both conditions share underlying gut-related biology, pointing toward shared treatment strategies such as gut microbiome repair.

papers.title: Development and validation of blood-based diagnostic biomarkers for Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome (ME/CFS) using EpiSwitch® 3-dimensional genomic regulatory immuno-genetic profiling
papers.authors: Hunter E, Alshaker H, Bundock O, Weston C, Bautista S, Gebregzabhar A, Virdi A, Croxford J, Dring A, Powell R, Vugrinec D, Kingdon C, Wilson C, Dowrick S, Green J, Akoulitchev A, Pchejetski D
papers.summary: Developed and validated a blood test using a novel technique that reads three-dimensional patterns of DNA folding; the test correctly identified ME/CFS patients versus healthy people with 96% accuracy in an independent test group.
papers.why: One of the highest-accuracy diagnostic blood tests ever reported for ME/CFS — a disease that currently has no objective diagnostic test — and reveals specific immune and brain-inflammation pathways as potential drug targets.

papers.title: Heightened innate immunity may trigger chronic inflammation, fatigue and post-exertional malaise in ME/CFS
papers.authors: Che X, Ranjan A, Guo C, Zhang K, Goldsmith R, Levine S, Moneghetti KJ, Zhai Y, Ge L, Mishra N, Hornig M, Bateman L, Klimas NG, Montoya JG, Peterson DL, Klein SL, Fiehn O, Komaroff AL, Lipkin WI
papers.summary: Found that immune cells from ME/CFS patients overreact to signals from bacteria and viruses — producing far more inflammation-driving molecules than healthy controls — and that this overreaction is linked to disrupted energy production and gut bacteria imbalances.
papers.why: A large multi-institutional study (led by Ian Lipkin at Columbia) that pins down overactive 'first-response' immunity as a key driver of ME/CFS symptoms, and identifies several existing drugs — including metformin and rapamycin — as possible treatments to test.

papers.title: Patient-reported treatment outcomes in ME/CFS and long COVID
papers.authors: Eckey M, Li P, Morrison B, Bergquist J, Davis RW, Xiao W
papers.summary: A survey of nearly 4,000 patients across ME/CFS and long COVID asked what helped them from over 150 treatments; people with both conditions reported remarkably similar responses — and graded exercise therapy was rated least helpful.
papers.why: The largest real-world treatment survey in ME/CFS/long COVID, showing the two conditions behave similarly and pointing to which symptom subtypes (e.g. autonomic, cognitive) respond best to which drug classes — directly useful for designing future trials.

papers.title: Pathophysiological, Translational, and Diagnostic Aspects of ME/CFS: A Focus on Skeletal Muscle Involvement
papers.authors: Fanò-Illic G, Coscia F, Gigliotti PV, Checcaglini F, Carraro U, Fulle S, Mancinelli R
papers.summary: A detailed review of how muscles in ME/CFS fail — they receive too little blood, build up harmful levels of sodium and calcium, produce too much oxidative stress, and lose their normal ability to contract — and how these changes might be detected by standard tests.
papers.why: Integrates molecular, cellular, and clinical evidence into a single picture of why ME/CFS muscles don't work normally, and proposes markers that could be used for diagnosis — supporting the muscle-focused treatment approach being pursued by researchers including Wirth and Scheibenbogen.

papers.title: Hyperbaric oxygen therapy improves clinical symptoms and functional capacity and modulates thalamic connectivity in ME/CFS: a prospective cohort study
papers.authors: Kim L, Cammà G, Peters CK, Mantwill M, Müller O, Leprêtre N, Heindrich C, Rust R, Krill M, Hartung TJ, Reeß LG, Krohn S, Heymann CV, Wittke K, Finke C, Scheibenbogen C
papers.summary: ME/CFS patients who received hyperbaric oxygen treatment (breathing pure oxygen in a pressurised chamber) showed improved symptoms and physical function; brain scans also revealed changes in an area linked to fatigue.
papers.why: Involves Carmen Scheibenbogen's team and is one of the first studies to link symptom improvement with measurable brain changes after oxygen treatment — potentially pointing to both a therapy and a biological marker of response.

papers.title: Low Vasopressin in Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome
papers.authors: Huhmar HM, Soinne LS, Bertilson BC, Ghatan PH, Bragée BA, Polo OJ
papers.summary: Found that levels of vasopressin — a hormone that helps control water balance, blood flow, and stress responses — were lower in ME/CFS patients than in healthy people.
papers.why: Low vasopressin could help explain symptoms like dizziness, excessive thirst, and fatigue, and may become a diagnostic marker or a target for treatment.

papers.title: Dynamic microclot profiling: thromboelastography advances precision management in long COVID and myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome
papers.authors: Saleem S, Hussain A, Haroon M, Raza A, Afzal U, Anwar MF, Imran S, Iqbal MU, Hajj F
papers.summary: Used a specialised clotting test to measure tiny blood clots (microclots) in long COVID and ME/CFS patients, finding different clotting patterns among individuals that could guide personalised treatment.
papers.why: Adds to the growing evidence that abnormal clotting and poor circulation contribute to ME/CFS; the profiling method it describes could help identify which patients might benefit from anti-clotting therapies.

papers.title: Imbalance of Excitatory and Inhibitory Neurotransmitter Systems in Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome
papers.authors: Wirth KJ, Scheibenbogen C
papers.summary: Reviews evidence that ME/CFS disturbs the brain's chemical messengers — the signals nerve cells use to talk to each other — which may explain symptoms like brain fog and feeling 'wired but tired'.
papers.why: From the two leading German ME/CFS researchers: it links the disease's effects in the muscles to its effects in the brain, reinforcing the case for the treatments they are developing.

papers.title: Charting the circulating proteome in ME/CFS using cross-system profiling to uncover mechanistic insights
papers.authors: Hoel A, Hoel F, Dyrstad SE, Chapola H, Rekeland IG, Risa K, Alme K, Sørland K, Brokstad KA, Marti HP, Mella O, Fluge Ø, Tronstad KJ
papers.summary: Measured thousands of proteins in patients' blood and found many that differ from healthy people — signs of an overactive immune system and poor blood flow to the muscles.
papers.why: The largest blood-protein study in ME/CFS so far; it maps what is going wrong in the body and could lead to a blood test and new treatment targets.

papers.title: Proteomic signatures in cerebrospinal fluid and their clinical associations in patients with ME/CFS
papers.authors: Bragée B, Li P, Meadows D, et al.
papers.summary: Examined proteins in the fluid that bathes the brain and spinal cord, and found inflammation-related patterns that matched how severe patients were and whether they also had a racing heart on standing up (a condition called POTS).
papers.why: One of the first studies of this fluid in ME/CFS; the patterns it found could become markers for sorting patients into the right treatment groups.

papers.title: Pathophysiological mechanisms of post-exertional malaise: an integrative analysis based on the metabolism-immune-neuro interaction model
papers.authors: Jin H, An Y, Huang J, Luo T, Wu X
papers.summary: Proposes how even light activity can trigger a 'crash' (the delayed worsening of symptoms after exertion, called post-exertional malaise): the cells' energy units falter, this sparks inflammation, and the inflammation reaches the brain and keeps the cycle going.
papers.why: Offers a clear, testable explanation for ME/CFS's defining symptom — crashing after exertion — and points toward energy- and inflammation-based treatments.

papers.title: Elevated serum levels of interleukin-11 and matrix metalloproteinase-9 in myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome
papers.authors: Chinnappan B, Kempuraj D, Aenlle KK, Middleton A, Day KS, Kothuru SP, Joshi RS, Klimas NG, Theoharides TC
papers.summary: Found two inflammation-related substances raised in patients' blood, and linked them to immune cells reacting to the Epstein–Barr virus (the cause of glandular fever).
papers.why: These substances could serve as a blood marker, and the link to a common virus hints at new treatment ideas.

papers.title: Large-scale investigation confirms TRPM3 ion channel dysfunction in Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome
papers.authors: Sasso EM, Er TS, Eaton-Fitch N, Hool L, Muraki K, Marshall-Gradisnik S
papers.summary: Confirmed, across several laboratories, that a tiny 'gate' which lets calcium into certain immune cells doesn't work properly in ME/CFS patients.
papers.why: Because independent labs got the same result, this faulty cell 'gate' is one of the most reliable biological signs of ME/CFS and a possible drug target.

papers.title: Plasma cell targeting with the anti-CD38 antibody daratumumab in myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome — a clinical pilot study
papers.authors: Fluge Ø, Rekeland IG, Sørland K, Alme K, Risa K, Bruland O, Tronstad KJ, Mella O
papers.summary: A small trial gave 10 patients daratumumab — a drug that clears the immune cells which make antibodies; 6 of 10 improved and several stayed better for one to two years.
papers.why: The first trial of this approach in ME/CFS; the strong, lasting improvement supports the idea that misguided antibodies drive the illness, and a larger trial is planned.

papers.title: Safety, tolerability and clinical effects of rovunaptabin (BC007) on fatigue and quality of life in patients with Post-COVID syndrome (reCOVer): a phase IIa randomised controlled trial
papers.authors: Hohberger B, Ganslmayer M, Harrer T, et al.
papers.summary: In a rigorous trial with a placebo group, a drug that neutralises harmful 'self-attacking' antibodies reduced fatigue and improved quality of life in Long COVID patients.
papers.why: The first properly controlled trial showing benefit from removing these antibodies — strong support for antibody-targeting treatments that are also being explored in ME/CFS.

papers.title: Key Pathophysiological Role of Skeletal Muscle Disturbance in Post COVID and Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome (ME/CFS): Accumulated Evidence
papers.authors: Scheibenbogen C, Wirth KJ
papers.journal: Journal of Cachexia, Sarcopenia and Muscle
papers.summary: Pulls together evidence that ME/CFS muscles get too little blood and build up salt (sodium) and calcium, which damages the muscle cells' energy units.
papers.why: A cornerstone of the Wirth/Scheibenbogen theory and the direct rationale for their drug MDC002, which aims to fix exactly this muscle problem.

papers.title: Expert perspectives on myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome — Insights from the 3rd International Conference of the Charité Fatigue Center
papers.authors: Fehrer A, et al.
papers.summary: Summarises what experts at Charité's international ME/CFS conference agree on — problems with circulation, the immune system, and the body's energy use — and warns that steady research funding is urgently needed.
papers.why: Lays out the Scheibenbogen group's current priorities and the thinking behind their trials (immunoadsorption and the planned inebilizumab study).

papers.title: Tryptophan Metabolism and Aryl-Hydrocarbon Receptor Agonists in the Gut Microbiome of People With Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome
papers.authors: Esteban DJ, Conrad B, Cullinan A, et al.
papers.summary: Found that the gut bacteria of ME/CFS patients handle the nutrient tryptophan differently, making fewer of the molecules that help keep the immune system calm.
papers.why: Points to the gut as a possible lever on the immune system, supporting microbiome- and immune-based treatment ideas.

papers.title: Systems neuroendocrinology in ME/CFS and long COVID: a chronobiological framework for hormone-based research
papers.authors: Thomas N, Huang K, Schneider-Futschik EK, et al.
papers.summary: Argues that hormone studies in ME/CFS and Long COVID disagree because they ignore differences by time of day and menstrual-cycle phase, and recommends accounting for these.
papers.why: Better-designed hormone studies could uncover treatment targets; gives practical guidance for trials, especially those with mostly female participants.

papers.title: Gastrointestinal symptoms correlate with core clinical features and systemic inflammation in myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome
papers.authors: Brown M, Vernon SD, Indart AC, Green PH, Alaedini A
papers.summary: Showed that the worse a patient's gut symptoms are, the worse their main ME/CFS symptoms and body-wide inflammation tend to be — suggesting the gut is part of the disease, not a side issue.
papers.why: Supports treating gut problems as central to ME/CFS, which could shape who is enrolled in drug trials and how improvement is measured.

papers.title: A Unifying Hypothesis of the Pathophysiology of Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome (ME/CFS): Recognitions from the finding of autoantibodies against ß2-adrenergic receptors
papers.authors: Wirth KJ, Scheibenbogen C
papers.summary: Proposes one framework that ties ME/CFS together: 'self-attacking' antibodies against receptors that control blood vessels lead to poor blood flow and too little oxygen reaching muscles and brain.
papers.why: The central Wirth/Scheibenbogen theory — it underpins their drug MDC002 and the autoantibody-removal treatments now being tested.

papers.title: Pathophysiology of skeletal muscle disturbances in Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome (ME/CFS)
papers.authors: Wirth KJ, Scheibenbogen C
papers.summary: Explains in detail how poor blood flow and a buildup of sodium and calcium could damage how ME/CFS muscles make energy.
papers.why: Builds the muscle-level mechanism behind the Wirth/Scheibenbogen theory and the rationale for their drug MDC002.

papers.title: Antibodies to β adrenergic and muscarinic cholinergic receptors in patients with Chronic Fatigue Syndrome
papers.authors: Loebel M, Grabowski P, Heidecke H, et al. (Scheibenbogen C, senior author)
papers.summary: Found that many ME/CFS patients carry raised 'self-attacking' antibodies against receptors that control blood vessels and the nervous system.
papers.why: A foundational finding for the autoimmune theory of ME/CFS and the basis for treatments that remove these antibodies (immunoadsorption, BC007). From the Scheibenbogen group.

papers.title: Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome — Evidence for an autoimmune disease
papers.authors: Sotzny F, Blanco J, Capelli E, Castro-Marrero J, Steiner S, Murovska M, Scheibenbogen C (EUROMENE)
papers.summary: A review gathering the evidence that ME/CFS — at least in a subgroup of patients — behaves like an autoimmune disease, where the immune system attacks the body.
papers.why: Framed the autoimmune model that drives much of today's treatment research. From the Scheibenbogen group / European EUROMENE network.

papers.title: Endothelial dysfunction and altered endothelial biomarkers in patients with post-COVID-19 syndrome and chronic fatigue syndrome (ME/CFS)
papers.authors: Haffke M, Freitag H, Rudolf G, et al. (Scheibenbogen C, Sotzny F)
papers.summary: Found that the inner lining of blood vessels (the endothelium) doesn't work properly in many ME/CFS and Long COVID patients.
papers.why: Supports the idea that poor blood-vessel function drives ME/CFS symptoms, and points to treatments that target the vessels. From the Scheibenbogen group.

papers.title: Human Herpesvirus-6 Reactivation, Mitochondrial Fragmentation, and the Coordination of Antiviral and Metabolic Phenotypes in Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome
papers.authors: Schreiner P, Harrer T, Scheibenbogen C, Lamer S, Schlosser A, Naviaux RK, Prusty BK
papers.summary: Suggests that reactivation of a common herpesvirus (HHV-6) can shatter cells' energy factories (mitochondria), switching the body into a protective but energy-starved state.
papers.why: Connects a viral trigger to the energy failure seen in ME/CFS — a possible mechanism linking an old infection to lasting illness. Co-authored by Scheibenbogen, Naviaux and Prusty.

papers.title: Initial findings from the DecodeME genome-wide association study of myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome
papers.authors: Ponting CP, et al. (DecodeME Consortium)
papers.summary: The largest genetic study of ME/CFS (over 15,000 patients) found eight spots in our DNA linked to the illness, several involved in how the body fights infection.
papers.why: First strong genetic evidence that ME/CFS has a biological basis involving the immune and nervous systems — and a source of new drug targets.

papers.title: Deep phenotyping of post-infectious myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome
papers.authors: Walitt B, Singh K, et al. (NIH intramural study)
papers.summary: An intensive US National Institutes of Health study found measurable brain, immune and metabolic differences in people whose ME/CFS began after an infection.
papers.why: A high-profile government study confirming the illness has real biological abnormalities (though its 'effort preference' wording was controversial among patients).

papers.title: AI-driven multi-omics modeling of myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome
papers.authors: Xiong R, Aiken E, Caldwell R, Vernon SD, Kozhaya L, Gunter C, Bateman L, Unutmaz D, Oh J
papers.summary: Used artificial intelligence on four years of layered biological data (gut, blood, immune, metabolism) to pick out ME/CFS biomarkers and predict how severe symptoms are.
papers.why: A cutting-edge, data-driven map of the disease that could improve diagnosis and reveal new mechanisms.

papers.title: Circulating cell-free RNA signatures for the characterization and diagnosis of myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome
papers.authors: Gardella AE, Eweis-LaBolle D, Loy CJ, et al. (Hanson MR, De Vlaminck I)
papers.summary: A blood test reading fragments of genetic material (RNA) floating in plasma told ME/CFS patients from healthy people with about 77% accuracy, and flagged immune and platelet differences.
papers.why: A promising step toward an objective blood test for a disease that currently has none, plus fresh clues about immune involvement. Cornell (Hanson) group.

papers.title: Metabolic features of chronic fatigue syndrome
papers.authors: Naviaux RK, Naviaux JC, Li K, et al.
papers.summary: Found that ME/CFS patients' blood chemistry shows a widespread 'slowed-down' metabolism — the body acting as if in a low-energy survival mode.
papers.why: A widely-cited study arguing ME/CFS is a measurable disorder of how the body makes and uses energy, not a psychological problem.

papers.title: Metabolic profiling indicates impaired pyruvate dehydrogenase function in myalgic encephalopathy/chronic fatigue syndrome
papers.authors: Fluge Ø, Mella O, Bruland O, et al.
papers.summary: Blood chemistry pointed to a blocked step in how cells turn food into energy — a key enzyme (pyruvate dehydrogenase) appears to be held back.
papers.why: A concrete, testable defect in energy production that could explain the exhaustion and serve as a drug target. Bergen (Fluge/Mella) group.

papers.title: Cytokine signature associated with disease severity in chronic fatigue syndrome patients
papers.authors: Montoya JG, Holmes TH, Anderson JN, et al. (Davis MM)
papers.summary: Levels of several immune-signaling molecules (cytokines) in the blood rose in step with how severe a patient's ME/CFS was.
papers.why: Links the immune system directly to symptom severity and points to inflammation-based markers and treatments. Stanford group.

papers.title: Distinct plasma immune signatures in ME/CFS are present early in the course of illness
papers.authors: Hornig M, Montoya JG, Klimas NG, et al. (Lipkin WI)
papers.summary: Patients ill for under three years had a distinctive blood immune pattern that faded in those ill longer — showing the disease has stages.
papers.why: Early hard evidence that ME/CFS is biological and changes over time, and a hint that treating early might matter. Columbia (Lipkin) group.

papers.title: Neuroinflammation in Patients with Chronic Fatigue Syndrome/Myalgic Encephalomyelitis: An 11C-(R)-PK11195 PET Study
papers.authors: Nakatomi Y, Mizuno K, Ishii A, et al.
papers.summary: Brain scans showed signs of inflammation (activated immune cells) in several brain regions of ME/CFS patients.
papers.why: Among the first direct evidence of brain inflammation in ME/CFS, supporting the 'encephalomyelitis' (brain inflammation) part of the disease's name.

papers.title: Insights From Invasive Cardiopulmonary Exercise Testing of Patients With Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome
papers.authors: Joseph P, Arevalo C, Oliveira RKF, et al. (Systrom DM)
papers.summary: Using exercise tests with catheters in the blood vessels, found that ME/CFS patients' blood doesn't return and circulate properly during exertion ('preload failure'), alongside nerve abnormalities.
papers.why: Gives a physical, measurable explanation for exercise intolerance and points to circulation- and nerve-targeted treatments. Harvard (Systrom) group.

papers.title: Inability of myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome patients to reproduce VO2peak indicates functional impairment
papers.authors: Keller BA, Pryor JL, Giloteaux L
papers.summary: On two exercise tests 24 hours apart, ME/CFS patients could not match their day-1 performance on day 2 — an objective sign of the 'crash' after exertion.
papers.why: Provides an objective, measurable test of post-exertional malaise — important for diagnosis and disability assessment.

papers.title: Reduced diversity and altered composition of the gut microbiome in individuals with myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome
papers.authors: Giloteaux L, Goodrich JK, Walters WA, Levine SM, Ley RE, Hanson MR
papers.summary: ME/CFS patients had less varied gut bacteria and signs of a 'leaky' gut letting microbes into the bloodstream.
papers.why: Early evidence linking the gut microbiome to ME/CFS, opening microbiome-based diagnosis and treatment ideas. Cornell (Hanson) group.

papers.title: Multi-'omics of gut microbiome-host interactions in short- and long-term myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome patients
papers.authors: Xiong R, Gunter C, Fleming E, et al. (Hanson MR, Oh J, Unutmaz D)
papers.summary: A detailed gut analysis showed ME/CFS patients lack bacteria that make butyrate (a fuel that nourishes the gut lining); the pattern differed depending on how long they had been ill.
papers.why: One of the most rigorous microbiome studies in ME/CFS, strengthening the gut-disease link and possible treatments.

papers.title: A nanoelectronics-blood-based diagnostic biomarker for myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome (ME/CFS)
papers.authors: Esfandyarpour R, Kashi A, Nemat-Gorgani M, Wilhelmy J, Davis RW
papers.summary: A tiny electronic 'nanoneedle' blood test separated ME/CFS patients from healthy people by how their cells cope with stress.
papers.why: A candidate objective diagnostic test for a disease that currently has none. Stanford / Open Medicine Foundation (Davis) group.

papers.title: B-Lymphocyte Depletion in Patients With Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome: A Randomized, Double-Blind, Placebo-Controlled Trial (RituxME)
papers.authors: Fluge Ø, Rekeland IG, Lien K, et al. (Mella O)
papers.summary: A large, rigorous trial of rituximab — a drug that wipes out B-cells (an immune cell type) — found it did NOT help ME/CFS patients overall.
papers.why: A pivotal negative result that ended hopes for rituximab and redirected the field; a landmark in ME/CFS research history. Bergen (Fluge/Mella) group.

papers.title: Intravenous Cyclophosphamide in Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome. An Open-Label Phase II Study
papers.authors: Rekeland IG, Fosså A, Lande A, et al. (Fluge Ø, Mella O)
papers.summary: An open-label study (no placebo group) of the immune-suppressing drug cyclophosphamide reported that about half of ME/CFS patients improved.
papers.why: An early signal that calming the immune system may help some patients — needs placebo-controlled confirmation, but supports the autoimmune treatment line.

papers.title: Insights from myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome may help unravel the pathogenesis of post-acute COVID-19 syndrome
papers.authors: Komaroff AL, Lipkin WI
papers.summary: A review arguing that Long COVID and ME/CFS likely share the same underlying biology.
papers.why: Helped place ME/CFS at the centre of Long COVID research, bringing the field new attention and funding.

papers.title: Myalgic encephalomyelitis: International Consensus Criteria
papers.authors: Carruthers BM, van de Sande MI, De Meirleir KL, et al.
papers.summary: An international expert panel defined stricter ways to diagnose the illness, centred on symptoms getting worse after exertion, and recommended dropping the name 'chronic fatigue syndrome'.
papers.why: Widely-used research and clinical criteria that put the disease's hallmark feature — post-exertional worsening — at the centre of diagnosis.

papers.title: Beyond Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome: Redefining an Illness
papers.authors: Institute of Medicine (US) Committee (National Academies)
papers.summary: A major US expert panel concluded ME/CFS is a serious physical disease, proposed simpler diagnostic criteria, and suggested renaming it 'systemic exertion intolerance disease' (SEID).
papers.why: A landmark report that medically legitimised the illness and reshaped how it is diagnosed.

papers.title: Systematic review and meta-analysis of the prevalence of chronic fatigue syndrome/myalgic encephalomyelitis (CFS/ME)
papers.authors: Lim EJ, Ahn YC, Jang ES, et al.
papers.summary: Pooled dozens of studies to estimate how common ME/CFS is worldwide (roughly 0.5–1% of people, depending on the definition used).
papers.why: Establishes the scale of the disease — many millions affected — strengthening the case for research funding and services.

papers.title: Immunoglobulin G complexes from post-infectious ME/CFS, including post-COVID ME/CFS disrupt cellular energetics and alter inflammatory marker secretion
papers.authors: Liu Z, Hollmann C, Kalanidhi S, Lamer S, Schlosser A, Basens EE, Nikolayshvili G, Sokolovska L, Riemekasten G, Rust R, Bellmann-Strobl J, Paul F, Naviaux RK, Nora-Krukle Z, Sotzny F, Scheibenbogen C, Prusty BK
papers.summary: Antibodies (immune proteins called IgG) taken from ME/CFS patients' blood were shown to shatter the energy-producing structures inside blood-vessel cells and change the cells' inflammatory signals.
papers.why: From Scheibenbogen and Prusty: directly demonstrates that ME/CFS antibodies damage cellular energy machinery — supporting antibody-removing treatments such as immunoadsorption and highlighting why patients' blood vessels may not function normally.

papers.title: Extracellular Vesicle Protein and MiRNA Signatures as Biomarkers for Post-Infectious ME/CFS Patients
papers.authors: Seifert M, Schäfers J, Douglas FF, Schwarzburg C, Boristowski D, Birke A, Klein O, Sotzny F, Rubarth K, Windzio L, Beez CM, Kedor Peters C, Wittke K, Scheibenbogen C, Greco A
papers.summary: Tiny particles shed by blood cells (extracellular vesicles) carry distinctive protein and genetic signals in ME/CFS patients, and lower levels of one small genetic molecule (microRNA let-7b-5p) were linked to worse fatigue and pain.
papers.why: From the Scheibenbogen group: these vesicle-based markers could become a blood test for ME/CFS and help separate patients into subgroups for more targeted treatment.

papers.title: A causal link between autoantibodies and neurological symptoms in long COVID
papers.authors: Santos Guedes de Sá K, Silva J, Bayarri-Olmos R, et al. (Iwasaki A, senior author)
papers.summary: Antibodies produced during COVID infection persist in long COVID patients and, when transferred into mice, caused fatigue, coordination problems, and nerve damage — proving these 'self-attacking' antibodies directly cause neurological symptoms.
papers.why: High-impact proof (in Cell, one of science's top journals) that autoantibodies physically cause post-viral illness — directly supports the antibody-removing treatments being developed for ME/CFS.

papers.title: The potential causes of myasthenia and fasciculations in severely ill ME/CFS patients: the role of disturbed electrophysiology
papers.authors: Wirth KJ, Steinacker JM
papers.summary: Klaus Wirth proposes that muscle weakness, cramps, and twitching in severely ill ME/CFS patients all arise from one underlying problem: an energy pump in muscle membranes fails, triggering a cascade of excess calcium and electrical instability.
papers.why: From Wirth (Mitodicure): the mechanism described is exactly what his drug MDC002 aims to fix, making this a direct scientific rationale for the planned MDC002 Phase 1 trial.

papers.title: Irisin Signaling Resistance in Myalgic Encephalomyelitis: A Proposed Mechanistic Framework for Post-Exertional Malaise Involving the TSP-1-HSP90α-αvβ5 Axis
papers.authors: Souma B, Elremaly W, Akoume MY, Elbakry M, Godbout C, Moreau A
papers.summary: ME patients had lower levels of irisin (a hormone normally released by exercise) and an abnormal response when it was present, blocked by another protein called thrombospondin-1 — pointing to a new molecular explanation for why any exertion triggers a crash.
papers.why: Identifies a previously unknown pathway behind post-exertional malaise and suggests thrombospondin-1 and its signalling partners as possible targets for drugs that reduce the crash after activity.

papers.title: Raman Spectroscopy Combined with Machine Learning Reveals Myalgic Encephalomyelitis-Associated Biomolecular Signatures at Rest and After Standardized Stress
papers.authors: Heidarifard M, Moezzi A, Dallaire F, Ember K, Elremaly W, Caraus I, Franco A, Leblond F, Moreau A, Dehaes M
papers.summary: A light-based test (Raman spectroscopy) combined with AI correctly distinguished ME patients from healthy people about 80–85% of the time using chemical changes in blood plasma, both at rest and after a standardised stress test.
papers.why: Demonstrates a rapid, potentially low-cost screening tool for a disease that currently has no objective diagnostic test, and shows the test still works after exertion — relevant for detecting patients whose symptoms worsen with activity.

papers.title: Long-Term Follow-Up of Women with Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome (ME/CFS): A 16-Year Longitudinal Study
papers.authors: Tomić S, Pastornački A, Drljača M, Glogovac J, Bošković V, Brkić S
papers.summary: Following 20 women with ME/CFS for 16 years found that 85% developed significant additional illnesses — including rheumatoid arthritis, cancer, depression, and heart disease — showing the severe long-term health burden of untreated ME/CFS.
papers.why: Among the longest follow-up studies in ME/CFS; the high rate of additional illness underscores the urgency of effective treatment, and the pattern of which conditions develop may point to shared biological mechanisms.

papers.title: Two-timepoint multidomain follow-up of post-COVID condition and ME/CFS: overlapping autonomic, small-fiber, and cognitive changes
papers.authors: Azcue N, Barranco C, Tijero-Merino B, Acera M, Fernández-Valle T, Lafuente JV, Gabilondo I, Ruiz-Lopez M, Del Pino R, Gómez-Esteban JC
papers.summary: Tracking both post-COVID and ME/CFS patients over nearly three years showed their nerve, blood-pressure regulation, and thinking-speed changes were largely the same — and that post-COVID patients increasingly resembled established ME/CFS over time.
papers.why: Provides direct longitudinal evidence that post-COVID and ME/CFS share the same underlying disease process, strengthening the case for treatments that would work for both conditions.

papers.title: Exploring a genetic basis for the metabolic perturbations in ME/CFS using UK biobank
papers.authors: Huang K, et al.
papers.summary: Using UK Biobank data from nearly 37,000 people, researchers linked specific gene variants to blood chemistry abnormalities in ME/CFS patients, pointing to disrupted fat metabolism and neurotransmitter (brain chemical) pathways as genetically influenced features of the disease.
papers.why: A genome-wide analysis of ME/CFS metabolism in one of the world's largest health datasets — the genetic connections it finds could point to drug targets and help explain why patients' cells behave differently from healthy people.

papers.title: Reframing ME/CFS: toward a unified mechanistic model of chronic post-infectious diseases
papers.authors: Watton P, Prusty BK
papers.summary: A detailed review tracing how ME/CFS research shifted from uncertainty to a clear biological picture: advances in long COVID research revealed multiple converging faults — immune disruption, energy-factory failure in cells, damaged blood vessels, abnormal blood clotting — which often only become visible when the body is put under physical stress.
papers.why: From Prusty (whose lab showed that ME/CFS patients' antibodies can shatter cell energy machinery): this unified framework pulls together all major biological findings and argues that stress-revealed pathology should drive how trials are designed and which patients are selected — potentially making future treatments much more targeted.
